metadata.scope: Definition-focused retrieval, distinction, and application questions, with coverage in every unit.

questions.prompt: Classify the function as linear, affine but not linear, or neither, and justify your answer.
hints: A linear function has zero constant term.
questions.fullSolution: The variables appear only to the first power and are not multiplied, so the function is affine. The constant term is 7 rather than 0, so it is not linear under the textbook definition.
questions.finalAnswer: Affine but not linear.
questions.finalAnswerLatex: f\text{ is affine but not linear}
questions.learningObjective: Distinguish affine and linear functions

questions.prompt: An LP has variables x1,x2,x3, but only x1 and x3 are required to be integers. Is the resulting IP pure or mixed?
hints: Ask whether every variable is integer-constrained.
questions.fullSolution: At least one variable is integer-constrained, so the model is an IP. Because x2 remains continuous, not every variable is required to be integer, so it is mixed rather than pure.
questions.finalAnswer: It is a mixed integer program.
questions.learningObjective: Distinguish pure and mixed integer programs

questions.prompt: An LP's constraints have no common solution. Classify the outcome and explain why it is not unboundedness.
hints: Unboundedness requires feasible solutions with arbitrarily improving values.
questions.fullSolution: The LP is infeasible because its feasible region is empty. It is not unbounded: an unbounded LP must have feasible solutions whose values improve without a finite limit.
questions.finalAnswer: Infeasible, not unbounded.
questions.learningObjective: Distinguish infeasibility from unboundedness

questions.prompt: A_B is square and nonsingular, and its basic solution has x_B=(2,−1)^T with x_N=0. Which of the terms basis, basic solution, feasible basis, and BFS apply?
hints: A basis is defined by invertibility; feasibility additionally requires nonnegativity.
questions.fullSolution: B is a basis because A_B is square and nonsingular. The associated vector is its basic solution. The negative component makes the basic solution infeasible, so B is not a feasible basis and the vector is not a BFS.
questions.finalAnswer: Basis and basic solution apply; feasible basis and BFS do not.
questions.finalAnswerLatex: B\text{ is a basis but not a feasible basis}
questions.learningObjective: Separate basis terminology from feasibility

questions.prompt: A simplex pivot has minimum-ratio step length zero. What is this iteration called, and what can change even though the point does not?
hints: The basis and the basic feasible solution are not the same object.
questions.fullSolution: The iteration is degenerate. The entering and leaving variables change the basis, but the basic feasible solution and objective value remain unchanged for that iteration.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: B'\ne B,\qquad x'=x,\qquad c^{\mathsf T}x'=c^{\mathsf T}x
questions.finalAnswer: A degenerate iteration; the basis changes while the BFS may stay the same.
questions.learningObjective: Recognize degeneracy and its effect

questions.prompt: Under the course negative-sum maximization convention, Phase I has optimal value zero. What does this prove and what is retained for Phase II?
hints: At value zero, every nonnegative auxiliary variable can equal zero.
questions.fullSolution: The original LP is feasible. An optimal auxiliary solution with all auxiliary variables zero yields a feasible solution—and after cleaning the basis, a feasible basis—for the original variables. Remove auxiliary variables and restore the original objective for Phase II.
questions.finalAnswer: The original LP is feasible; retain an original-variable feasible basis for Phase II.
questions.learningObjective: Interpret the Phase I optimum

questions.prompt: A point x in a convex set can be written as a strict convex combination of two distinct points u and v in the set. Can x be an extreme point?
hints: Use the definition involving a line segment that properly contains x.
questions.fullSolution: No. The line segment joining u and v lies in the convex set and properly contains x, which contradicts the definition of an extreme point.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: u,v\in C,\ u\ne v,\ 0<\lambda<1\implies x\text{ is not extreme}
questions.finalAnswer: No; x is not an extreme point.
questions.learningObjective: Apply the definition of extreme point

questions.prompt: Write the dual of a maximization LP in standard equality form.
hints: A primal equality produces a free dual variable; a nonnegative primal variable produces a ≥ dual constraint.
questions.fullSolution: There is one free dual variable for each primal equality. Each nonnegative primal variable produces a dual constraint with direction ≥, and the dual objective uses the primal right-hand side.
questions.finalAnswer: Minimize b^T y subject to A^T y≥c, with y free.
questions.learningObjective: Construct the dual of an SEF maximization LP

questions.prompt: Find the cone of tight constraints at the given feasible point.
hints: Evaluate all three inequalities and keep only those holding with equality.
questions.fullSolution: At x-bar, the first and second constraints are tight, while x2≤2 has slack one. The tight normal vectors are (1,0)^T and (1,1)^T, so their nonnegative combinations form the cone.
questions.finalAnswer: The cone is generated by the first two constraint normals.
questions.learningObjective: Build the cone of tight constraints

questions.prompt: For two maximization problems with the same objective, S1 is contained in S2. Which problem is the relaxation, and how do their optimal values compare?
hints: The relaxation has the larger feasible region.
questions.fullSolution: The problem over S2 is a relaxation of the problem over S1. Maximizing over more points cannot produce a smaller optimum, so the relaxation value is an upper bound on the original value.
questions.finalAnswer: P2 is the relaxation and v(P2)≥v(P1).
questions.learningObjective: Interpret a maximization relaxation bound

questions.prompt: Use the definition to verify that s=2 is a subgradient of g at x-bar=1.
hints: Substitute into g(x-bar)+s(x−x-bar)≤g(x) and rearrange.
questions.fullSolution: The affine lower bound is 1+2(x−1)=2x−1. Comparing it with x^2 leaves x^2−2x+1=(x−1)^2, which is nonnegative for every real x.
questions.finalAnswer: The subgradient inequality holds for every x, so s=2 is a subgradient at 1.
questions.learningObjective: Verify a subgradient from its definition

questions.prompt: A model has affine constraints and the displayed objective. Classify it as an LP, a quadratic optimization problem, and/or an NLP.
hints: An LP requires an affine objective; a quadratic objective is nonlinear unless its quadratic part vanishes.
questions.fullSolution: The objective contains degree-two terms, so it is not affine and the model is not an LP. With affine constraints and a quadratic objective, it is a quadratic optimization problem. It is also an NLP because the objective is nonlinear.
questions.finalAnswer: It is a quadratic optimization problem and an NLP, but not an LP.
questions.learningObjective: Classify a quadratic model from its objective and constraints

questions.prompt: For a maximization LP, a feasible point has value 12 and a valid upper-bound certificate proves that every feasible value is at most 12. What conclusion follows, and why are both pieces needed?
questions.promptLatex: \bar x\in S,\quad c^{\mathsf T}\bar x=12,\quad v(P)\le12
hints: Feasibility supplies a lower bound on a maximization optimum.
questions.fullSolution: Because x-bar is feasible, its value cannot exceed the optimum, so 12 is a lower bound on v(P). The certificate supplies the matching upper bound. Together they squeeze the optimal value to 12 and prove x-bar is optimal. Either fact alone would leave one side of the argument open.
questions.finalAnswer: The point is optimal and the optimal value is 12.
questions.learningObjective: Recognize a matching-bound certificate of optimality

questions.prompt: Let B be a basis for Ax=b. Write the constraint system in canonical form for B and identify the basic solution.
hints: Premultiply the equality by the inverse of the basis matrix.
questions.fullSolution: Premultiplying by A_B inverse turns the basic columns into the identity. The canonical constraints are x_B plus A_B inverse A_N times x_N equals A_B inverse b. Setting nonbasic variables to zero gives the associated basic solution.
questions.finalAnswer: The canonical form has identity basic columns; the basic solution sets x_N=0 and x_B=A_B^{-1}b.
questions.learningObjective: Construct canonical form for a basis

questions.prompt: Under Bland's rule, x2 and x5 are eligible to enter. The minimum-ratio test ties between basic variables x3 and x4. Which variables enter and leave?
hints: Bland's rule selects the eligible variable with smallest index at each choice.
questions.fullSolution: Among eligible entering variables, x2 has the smallest index, so it enters. Among the tied leaving candidates, x3 has the smallest index, so it leaves. This deterministic tie-breaking rule prevents cycling.
questions.finalAnswer: x2 enters and x3 leaves.
questions.learningObjective: Apply Bland's anti-cycling rule

questions.prompt: After normalizing the right-hand side, Phase I adds the displayed auxiliary variable. Explain its role and give the immediate auxiliary basic feasible solution for this row.
hints: The auxiliary column is an identity column for this row.
questions.fullSolution: The auxiliary variable creates an obvious basic column. Taking u1 basic and the original variables nonbasic gives x1=x2=0 and u1=5, which satisfies the normalized row and nonnegativity. Phase I then tries to drive auxiliary variables to zero.
questions.finalAnswer: u1 supplies the initial basis; the immediate auxiliary BFS is (x1,x2,u1)=(0,0,5).
questions.learningObjective: Explain why auxiliary variables create an initial Phase I basis

questions.prompt: Classify the displayed set using the definitions of halfspace, polyhedron, and convex set. Is it bounded?
hints: One linear inequality defines a halfspace, and a finite intersection of halfspaces is a polyhedron.
questions.fullSolution: The set is a halfspace. Because a single halfspace is also a finite intersection of halfspaces, it is a polyhedron. Halfspaces are convex, so P is convex. It is unbounded because x1 and x2 can grow without limit while x1 remains nonnegative.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: P\text{ is a halfspace}\implies P\text{ is a convex polyhedron},\qquad P\text{ is unbounded}
questions.finalAnswer: P is an unbounded convex polyhedron (and specifically a halfspace).
questions.finalAnswerLatex: P\text{ is a halfspace, a polyhedron, convex, and unbounded}
questions.learningObjective: Relate halfspaces, polyhedra, convexity, and boundedness

questions.prompt: Write the dual of the displayed inequality-form maximization LP, including all sign restrictions.
hints: Each primal <= constraint creates a nonnegative dual variable; each nonnegative primal variable creates a >= dual constraint.
questions.fullSolution: There is one nonnegative dual variable for each primal inequality. The dual minimizes the right-hand-side vector against those variables, and its constraints are A transpose y at least c.
questions.finalAnswer: Minimize b^T y subject to A^T y>=c and y>=0.
questions.learningObjective: Construct the dual of an inequality-form maximization LP

questions.prompt: The vectors s and t are primal and dual slacks. Verify whether the displayed feasible primal-dual points satisfy complementary slackness.
questions.promptLatex: s=(0,3)^{\mathsf T},\ y=(4,0)^{\mathsf T},\qquad t=(2,0)^{\mathsf T},\ x=(0,5)^{\mathsf T}
hints: Check y_i s_i=0 for every primal constraint and x_j t_j=0 for every primal variable.
questions.fullSolution: For the primal constraints, the products are 4 times 0 and 0 times 3. For the dual constraints, they are 0 times 2 and 5 times 0. Every product is zero, so complementary slackness holds. With primal and dual feasibility, this certifies optimality.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: y\odot s=(0,0)^{\mathsf T},\qquad x\odot t=(0,0)^{\mathsf T}
questions.finalAnswer: Yes. All four complementary products are zero.
questions.learningObjective: Verify complementary slackness from primal and dual slacks

questions.prompt: An inequality is valid for every integer-feasible point but violated by the current LP-relaxation solution. What is it called, and what must adding it preserve?
hints: The inequality should remove the current fractional point without removing any point in the integer feasible set.
questions.fullSolution: The inequality is a cutting plane (or cut) for the current relaxation point. It separates the fractional point from the integer-feasible set. Because it is valid for every integer-feasible point, adding it preserves all feasible integer solutions while tightening the relaxation.
questions.finalAnswer: It is a cutting plane; it must preserve every integer-feasible point.
questions.learningObjective: Recognize a valid cutting plane

questions.prompt: For a convex problem with inequality constraints g1(x)<=0 and g2(x)<=0, a feasible point has the displayed values. Is it a Slater point?
hints: Slater's condition requires strict satisfaction of every inequality constraint.
questions.fullSolution: The point is feasible because both values are nonpositive, but it is not a Slater point. The second inequality is tight rather than strict. A Slater point must satisfy g_i(x-bar)<0 for every inequality index i.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: g_2(\bar x)=0\not<0\implies\bar x\text{ is not a Slater point}
questions.finalAnswer: No. The point is feasible but not Slater because the second constraint is not strict.
questions.learningObjective: Distinguish feasibility from strict Slater feasibility

questions.prompt: A cafe needs at least 5 workers for a shift but has at most 3 available. Is the model infeasible or unbounded?
hints: Ask whether any number can satisfy both rules at once.
questions.fullSolution: No value of x is both at least 5 and at most 3. The feasible set is empty, so the model is infeasible. Unboundedness would require feasible choices whose objective keeps improving; here there are no feasible choices at all.
questions.finalAnswer: The model is infeasible, not unbounded.
questions.learningObjective: Recognize infeasibility in a practical constraint conflict

questions.prompt: A warehouse can hold 10 pallets. Convert x1+x2≤10 to an equality with a slack variable, and explain what the slack means.
hints: Used capacity plus unused capacity equals total capacity.
questions.fullSolution: Add a nonnegative slack variable s. The equality is x1+x2+s=10. The value of s is the number of empty pallet spaces, so s=0 means the warehouse is full.
questions.finalAnswer: Use x1+x2+s=10 with s≥0; s is unused capacity.
questions.learningObjective: Interpret a slack variable as unused capacity

questions.prompt: A feasible production plan for a maximization LP earns 8. A feasible dual price plan costs 10. What does weak duality prove, and does it prove either plan is optimal?
questions.promptLatex: c^{\mathsf T}\bar x=8,\qquad b^{\mathsf T}\bar y=10
hints: A primal feasible value is a lower bound; a dual feasible value is an upper bound.
questions.fullSolution: Weak duality places the unknown optimum between the two feasible values: 8≤v(P)≤10. The values do not match, so this information alone does not prove that either candidate is optimal.
questions.finalAnswer: The optimal value lies between 8 and 10; optimality is not yet proved.
questions.learningObjective: Use primal and dual feasible values as lower and upper bounds

questions.prompt: At an optimal primal-dual pair, a resource constraint has 3 units of slack. What must its nonnegative dual variable equal?
hints: Complementary slackness says the dual variable times the primal slack is zero.
questions.fullSolution: Complementary slackness requires yi(bi-ai^T x-bar)=0. The slack equals 3, which is positive, so the only way the product can be zero is yi=0.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: \bar y_i\bigl(b_i-a_i^{\mathsf T}\bar x\bigr)=0,\quad b_i-a_i^{\mathsf T}\bar x=3\implies\bar y_i=0
questions.finalAnswer: The dual variable must be zero.
questions.learningObjective: Interpret complementary slackness as zero price for an unused resource

questions.prompt: A convex optimization problem has a locally optimal feasible point. What stronger conclusion can you draw?
questions.promptLatex: \bar x\text{ is locally optimal in a convex problem}
hints: Picture a bowl-shaped objective over a convex feasible region.
questions.fullSolution: For a convex optimization problem, a locally optimal feasible point is globally optimal. If a better feasible point existed elsewhere, the line segment toward it would create nearby feasible points with better values, contradicting local optimality.
questions.fullSolutionLatex: \bar x\text{ locally optimal}\implies\bar x\text{ globally optimal}
questions.finalAnswer: The point is globally optimal.
questions.learningObjective: Relate local and global optimality in convex optimization
